title: Swedish rye crispbread
subtitle: Thin, crisp rye crackers baked until bone dry
excerpt: Twelve large rye crispbreads rolled very thin and baked until fully dry. No yeast, no fat, no fuss. They keep for months in a tin and carry smoked salmon, cream cheese, or mature cheddar without buckling.
sourceNotes: Scandinavian crispbread (knäckebröd) traditions documented in pre-1928 Swedish and Norwegian cookery manuscripts, available through digitised Nordic library archives. The basic method of rolling rye dough thin and drying in a slow oven is unchanged across centuries. Cross-referenced with early twentieth-century Scandinavian homemaking guides.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Bake in two batches if the oven cannot fit two trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Crispbreads keep in an airtight tin for up to 3 months.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 210°C. Roll thin; bake until crackling dry and pale gold.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flatbread baked completely dry so it snaps rather than bends. The aim is to drive out all moisture; the cracker is done when it sounds hollow when tapped and shows no flex. Most crispbreads are leavened with water alone (the steam from the rye starch does some lifting), though some traditional recipes use a small amount of sourdough starter or bicarbonate of soda to lighten the crumb slightly.

content.text: are thin, snapping rye crackers made from rye flour and water with a little salt. The dough mixes in under five minutes and the baking is a single slow pass in the oven. Twelve large crackers, each about 20 by 12 cm. They keep for months in a tin.

Combine both flours, the salt, and caraway seeds (if using) in a bowl. Add the cold water and mix until the dough comes together into a smooth, firm ball. It will be stiffer than bread dough and will not be sticky. If it cracks when pressed, add water a tablespoon at a time.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan (210°C conventional) and line two large baking trays with paper. Divide the dough into 12 pieces. On a lightly floured surface, roll each piece to 2 mm or thinner; the crispbread should be nearly translucent at the edges. Lift carefully onto the lined trays, two or three per tray. Prick all over with a fork to prevent blistering.

Bake for 20 to 25 minutes, turning the trays halfway, until the crispbreads are pale gold and completely rigid. A crispbread that flexes needs another 5 minutes. Remove from the oven and let them cool flat on the trays; they will crisp further as they cool. Store in an airtight tin once fully cold.
